invariant_definition: Minute or atomic: a category of size and ontological minimality rather than a reference to modern physical atoms. In Vedānta the term often concerns the *jīva*, not gross matter. Viśiṣṭādvaita and Tattva-vāda (Dvaita) insist that the individual self is *aṇu* so that embodiment, karmic individuation, and plurality are real. The reach of cognition is then explained not by the self's size but by a further apparatus: *dharmabhūta-jñāna* in Viśiṣṭādvaita, or divinely ordered cognition in Tattva-vāda. In Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika and related atomisms, by contrast, *aṇu* and *paramāṇu* name the smallest material constituents and the atomic *manas* that mediates episodic awareness. The same word thus spans debates about conscious-substance size and about physical constitution. It is best read together with *vibhu*, *parimāṇa*, *paramāṇu*, and the dispute over whether a self can be all-pervading while still having body-specific experience.

per_school.definition: For Advaita, *aṇutva* cannot belong to the *ātman* in its own nature, since Gauḍapāda presents the self as ākāśavat and denies that the *jīva* is either a real part or a real modification of it [[MK 3.3-7](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.3-7)]. The apparently differentiated *jīva* belongs to *māyā* and *upacāra*; the unborn reality does not truly become many, so minute measure has no *pāramārthika* purchase here [[MK 3.19-21](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/3.Advaita-prakaraṇa.19-21); [MK 1.16-18](cite://gaudapada/mandukya-karika/1.Āgama-prakaraṇa.16-18)].

In the canonized Yāmuna locus, the *ātman* is not described as *aṇu* but as *nitya*, *vyāpī*, and *pratikṣetra-bhinna*; individuation is thus secured without reducing the self to minute measure [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/3)]. The indexed Rāmānuja passages accordingly place the weight on the self's distinction from body and on its ordered relation to the *antaryāmin*, not on intrinsic atomicity as the school's load-bearing account of *ātma-svarūpa* [[ĀS 3](cite://yamuna/atma-siddhi/3); [VedS §4](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§4); [VedS §6](cite://ramanuja/vedartha-samgraha/§6)].

The indexed Tattva-vāda loci make the decisive point through *bheda*: the *jīva* is irreducibly distinct from Īśvara and from other selves, and that plurality is real and beginningless within the *bhedapañcaka* [[Anuvy. 1.4.111-112](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/1.4.111-112)]. Scriptural *abheda*-language is therefore taken as resting on *sādṛśya*, *prādhānya*, or dependent designation, never on *svarūpābhidā*; the self cannot be a vibhu Brahman under this hermeneutic [[Anuvy. 2.3.66-69](cite://madhva/anuvyakhyana/2.3.66-69)].

Jīva Gosvāmī explains the *jīva*-aggregate as standing to Bhagavān by his *svābhāvika* and *acintya-śakti* in the manner of the quality of his atomic rays, so that *abheda* must be read only together with real distinction [[SatS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)]. The minute status of the selves is thus subordinated to the *śakti-śaktimat* relation: they are conscious and dependent, neither sheer *svarūpaikya* nor sheer external otherness [[SatS §42](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§42); [SatS §43](cite://jiva-gosvami/sat-sandarbha/§43)].

Nimbārka states the matter directly: the *jīva* is *aṇu*, *pratideha-bhinna*, possessed of *jñātṛtva*, and yet *hareḥ adhīna* [[DaŚ 1](cite://nimbarka/dasa-shloki/1)]. The school's *aṃśa*-reading keeps this minuteness within *svābhāvika-bhedābheda*: the self is really distinct, yet non-different as Brahman's dependent part and eternal emanational mode [[BS 2.3.42](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.42); [BS 2.3.43](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/2.3.43); [Gītā 15.7a](cite://nimbarka/vedanta-parijata-saurabha/15.7a)].

per_school.not_applicable_reason: In the indexed Vallabha materials, the jīva is doctrinally handled as Brahman's aṃśa within avikṛta-pariṇāma rather than as an atomically or minutely measured self [[BSB 2.3.43-45](cite://vallabha/anu-bhasya/2.3.43-45)].

per_school.definition: In Sāṃkhya, aṇutva does not characterize the puruṣa: the Kārikā tradition explicitly describes puruṣa as vyāpī, niravayava, and unlike the vyakta-complex, while the Sūtras assign atomic measure instead to the transmigrating liṅgaśarīra [[SKMV 11](cite://isvarakrsna/samkhya-karika-commentary/11); [KapSS 3.14](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/3.14)]. The same Sūtra collection denies vyāpakatva to manas, so minuteness belongs to the subtle psychical apparatus rather than to the witnessing self [[KapSS 5.69-71](cite://kapila/samkhya-sutra/5.69-71)].

In Yoga, paramāṇu marks the lower limit of contemplative mastery: citta can be steadied upon objects ranging from the atom to the greatest magnitude [[YBh on YS 1.40](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/1.40)]. Vyāsa also reports and rejects a body-sized, contractive-expansive theory of citta, insisting instead that citta is vibhu and that only its vṛttis contract and expand; accordingly Yoga does not make puruṣa or citta atomic in the ontological sense [[YBh on YS 4.10](cite://vyasa/yoga-bhasya/4.10)].

Nyāya-Vaiśeṣika treats aṇu as a basic parimāṇa opposed to mahat: the Vaiśeṣika-Sūtra defines aṇu by contrast with large magnitude and places atomicity at the level of imperceptible, eternal reals [[KVS 7.1.17-18](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.1.17-18)]. The same system distinguishes vibhu self from aṇu manas, and later compendia restate the point by calling the jīvātman pervasive while the inner organ remains paramāṇu-rūpa; cognition is episodic because this atomic mind must mediate self, sense, and object [[KVS 7.1.29-30](cite://kanada/vaisesika-sutra/7.1.29-30); [AnTs 17-18](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/17-18); [AnTs 25](cite://annambhatta/tarka-sangraha/25)].

In the available Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā corpus, aṇu appears not as a positive ontological primitive but as the target of Kumārila's critique of the Kāṇāda atomist appeal to viśeṣas inhering in same-sized atoms [[ŚV 5.9.29-30](cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/5.9.29-30)]. Kumārila argues that differentiated effects and manifest form already suffice for mutual exclusion, so an additional viśeṣa posited in atoms is explanatorily otiose [[ŚV 5.9.29-30](cite://kumarila/sloka-varttika/5.9.29-30)].

Vasubandhu argues that an external object cannot be established either as one whole, as many atoms, or as a collection of atoms, because the putative paramāṇu itself never becomes siddha [[Viṃśatikā 11](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/11)]. Once the atom fails, the aggregate fails as well, and the analysis of perceptual appearance is reassigned to vijñapti-mātra rather than to externally existing atomic matter [[Viṃśatikā 12-14](cite://vasubandhu/vimsatika/12-14)].

In Trika, aṇutva is not an ultimate size-category of consciousness but the contracted condition of the self under mala and śakti-saṃkoca: the bound being is called aṇu because universal powers are rendered parimita in the paśu-state [[PH 9](cite://ksemaraja/pratyabhijna-hrdayam/9)]. The IPV makes the point explicitly by saying that even elevated but non-pūrṇa agents remain aṇu so long as full kartṛtva is absent, and by locating the mukhya aṇutva of saṃsārins in the contracted, limited state of cittattva itself [[ĪPV on IPK 3.2.9](cite://abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.9); [ĪPV on IPK 3.2.11](cite://abhinavagupta/ipv/3.2.11)].

Jainism distinguishes atomic and aggregate forms of pudgala: matter is said to bear sensible qualities and to exist both as aṇu and as skandha, the latter arising through division and conjunction [[TS, pudgala section](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/pudgala-anavah-skandhas-ca)]. By contrast the jīva is not treated as an atom; its pradeśas contract and expand like a lamp's radiance, so aṇutva belongs here to matter rather than to selfhood [[TS, jīva-pradeśa section](cite://umasvati/tattvartha-sutra/jiva-pradesa-samhara-visarpa)].
primary_loci: Tattvārtha-Sūtra, pudgala section ('aṇavaḥ skandhāś ca' / 'bhedasaṃghātebhya utpadyante')

translator_note: The lazy gloss is 'atom.' Without qualification that invites modern physics and makes readers imagine a tiny material pellet. In classical usage *aṇu* is a size-category and often a metaphysical thesis about the self. Even where it does refer to material atoms, the issues are partlessness, combination, and causal constitution, not modern atomic theory.
